results.title: Griefing attack on seller’s airdrop benefits
results.reasoning: Yes. The report’s Item #14 (“Secondary Market Buyers Can Destroy Initial Minters' Airdrop Allocations”) matches the described issue. Both identify that TraitForgeNft.burn() subtracts airdrop weight from initialOwners[tokenId] instead of the current owner, enabling a buyer to burn/nuke a transferred token to reduce the original minter’s airdrop allocation. They reference the same code path (TraitForgeNft.burn() and NukeFund.nuke() calling burn) and the same attack scenario. A fix that changes the deduction to the current owner or migrates allocations on transfer would resolve both, so the vulnerability is the same and detected.

results.title: Incorrect percentage calculation in NukeFund and EntityForging when taxCut is changed from default value
results.reasoning: No finding in the audit addresses the taxCut percentage miscalculation in NukeFund.receive() or EntityForging.forgeWithListed(). While the report discusses NukeFund.receive() (DoS on failed transfers) and EntityForging.forgeWithListed() (excess ETH locked, wrong recipient), it never mentions that devShare/devFee is computed as amount/taxCut causing incorrect percentages when taxCut != 10, nor suggests a BPS-based fix. Fixes for the reported issues would not resolve the tax calculation flaw, so the described vulnerability was not detected.
